proposals.title: Differential Modulation of Fibroblast Proliferation and Tumor Cell Apoptosis by Calebin A in an Inflammatory Cytokine-Rich Microenvironment: Elucidating a Protective Mechanism for Normal Cells
proposals.motivation: The tumor microenvironment (TME) is rich in inflammatory cytokines that promote cancer progression, yet the effects of these cytokines and potential chemopreventive agents on normal stromal cells, like fibroblasts, remain poorly characterized. Pre-2026 work by Aggarwal's group shows Calebin A suppresses inflammation-associated oncogenic pathways in cancer cells, but preliminary data suggesting it augments fibroblast growth presents a critical paradox. Resolving this cell-type-specific duality is essential for understanding the full therapeutic potential and safety profile of dietary compounds in cancer prevention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Calebin A selectively modulates key inflammatory (NF-κB, STAT3) and survival (autophagy, PI3K/Akt) pathways in normal fibroblasts versus colorectal cancer cells, leading to cytokine-induced proliferative support in fibroblasts while inducing growth arrest and apoptosis in malignant cells.
proposals.method: We will establish co-cultures of primary human colonic fibroblasts and HCT-116 colorectal cancer cells exposed to a cytokine cocktail (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β). Using specific pathway inhibitors and GFP-LC3 reporter constructs, we will quantify autophagic flux (per autophagy guidelines 4th edition) and apoptosis (Annexin V/PI flow cytometry) in each cell type following Calebin A treatment. Western blotting and immunofluorescence will assess cell-type-specific activation states of NF-κB, STAT3, and Akt. RNA-seq will identify divergent transcriptional programs regulating proliferation versus death.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first mechanistic evidence for how a natural compound can simultaneously promote stromal resilience and exert anti-tumor activity within the same inflammatory TME. It will define a novel 'protective autophagy' signature in normal fibroblasts, advancing the field of chemoprevention beyond tumor cell-centric models. The findings will critically inform the development of TME-modulating strategies that spare normal tissue.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A's anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer effects are known, its potential to selectively enhance normal fibroblast viability under oncogenic stress is a novel and non-obvious finding. This proposal uniquely investigates the compound's dual role by directly comparing pathway modulation in paired normal and malignant cells from the same tissue, a approach not yet reported in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Dual-Phase Modulation of Wound Healing by TNF-α: Investigating Fibroblast Proliferation, Autophagic Flux, and the Therapeutic Potential of Calebin A in a Chronic Inflammation Model
proposals.motivation: TNF-α is a central inflammatory cytokine with a well-established but paradoxical role in cancer, promoting both cell survival and death. Its function in non-pathological tissue repair remains less defined, particularly its direct impact on fibroblast-driven wound closure. Given the known links between chronic inflammation, impaired healing, and cancer risk, understanding how TNF-α modulates fibroblast behavior is crucial for developing therapies that can steer inflammation towards resolution.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TNF-α exhibits a concentration-dependent, dual-phase effect on dermal fibroblasts, where low, acute levels augment proliferation and migration to aid repair, but sustained, high levels (as in chronic inflammation) induce autophagic dysfunction and a pro-fibrotic phenotype, which can be mitigated by the natural compound Calebin A.
proposals.method: We will use an in vitro scratch assay on primary human dermal fibroblasts treated with a gradient of TNF-α concentrations (0.1-20 ng/mL) over 72h to model acute vs. chronic exposure. Cell proliferation (MTT), migration (time-lapse microscopy), and key EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin, Snail via western blot) will be assessed. Autophagic flux will be monitored using the guidelines from the 4th edition autophagy assays, employing tandem mRFP-GFP-LC3 transfection and western blot for LC3-II/p62 under TNF-α with/without chloroquine. The therapeutic potential of Calebin A (5-20 µM) will be tested in the high-dose, chronic TNF-α model.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the specific conditions under which TNF-α transitions from a pro-healing to a tissue-damaging signal in fibroblasts, directly linking inflammatory dose/duration to repair outcomes. It will provide novel evidence on whether Calebin A, known for anti-tumor and anti-inflammatory effects, can repurpose autophagic pathways to resolve fibrosis and promote normal wound closure, suggesting a new therapeutic application.
proposals.novelty: While TNF-α's role in inflammation is known, its direct, phase-specific regulation of fibroblast function in wound healing is not fully mapped. Integrating this with autophagic flux analysis and testing Calebin A's efficacy in normalizing this process represents a novel convergence of Dr. Aggarwal's expertise in natural compounds, cancer biology, and inflammation-driven pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Cell Cycle Phase-Dependent Modulation of TNF-α-Induced Pro-Inflammatory Signaling and EMT by Calebin A in Colorectal Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: The pro-inflammatory cytokine TNF-α is a key driver of tumor progression, promoting inflammation, survival, and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in the tumor microenvironment. Pre-2026 evidence suggests cellular responses to external signals can vary dramatically across the cell cycle, but whether TNF-α's oncogenic effects are cell cycle phase-dependent remains unexplored. Determining this dependency could reveal novel chronotherapeutic targets to disrupt inflammation-driven cancer progression.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pro-inflammatory, pro-survival, and EMT-inducing effects of TNF-α on colorectal cancer (CRC) cells are potentiated in specific cell cycle phases, and that the natural compound Calebin A can selectively inhibit these phase-dependent responses.
proposals.method: We will apply well-established yeast-derived synchronization techniques adapted for mammalian cells, specifically double thymidine block and nocodazole arrest, to generate highly enriched populations of HCT-116 and/or SW480 CRC cells in G1/S, S, and G2/M phases. Synchronized cells will be treated with TNF-α, with or without pre-treatment with Calebin A. Phase-dependent effects will be quantified using: 1) Flow cytometry for cell cycle analysis and apoptosis (Annexin V/PI), 2) Western blotting for phase-specific markers (Cyclin B1, p-H3), NF-κB pathway activation (p-IκBα, p-p65), EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin, Snail), and autophagy flux (LC3-II/p62), and 3) qPCR for inflammatory gene expression (IL-6, IL-8, COX-2).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct link between cell cycle position and cellular susceptibility to TNF-α-driven oncogenic signaling in CRC. It will provide a mechanistic framework for understanding temporal vulnerabilities in inflammation-associated cancer progression and directly test the phase-specific efficacy of Calebin A, a promising chemopreventive agent.
proposals.novelty: While cell cycle synchronization is a classic technique, its application to dissect the phase-specificity of TNF-α's pleiotropic effects in cancer cells is novel. Furthermore, evaluating a natural compound's ability to block these phase-dependent signals integrates chronobiology with nutraceutical cancer prevention, a non-obvious extension of Dr. Aggarwal's prior work on Calebin A.

proposals.title: Calebin A Attenuates TNF-α-Induced Proliferation and Inflammatory Signaling in Primary Human Colorectal Tissue Explants: A Model Bridging Cell Lines to Complex Microenvironments
proposals.motivation: Pre-clinical cancer research heavily relies on immortalized cell lines, which lack the complex cellular heterogeneity and extracellular matrix interactions of native human tissue. This gap is critical when studying tumor-promoting inflammation, as the cytokine TNF-α exerts pleiotropic effects that are highly context-dependent within the tissue microenvironment. Validating anti-cancer compounds like Calebin A in a more physiologically relevant model is essential for translational relevance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in primary human colorectal tissue explants, TNF-α will induce a distinct proliferative and inflammatory program compared to cell lines, and that Calebin A will effectively suppress this program by modulating NF-κB signaling and associated autophagy pathways within the intact tissue architecture.
proposals.method: Fresh, non-malignant colorectal tissue specimens from elective surgeries will be cultured as 1-2 mm³ explants using established air-liquid interface or sponge-gel methods. Explants will be treated with TNF-α (10-20 ng/mL) with or without pre-/co-treatment with Calebin A (5-20 µM). Proliferation will be assessed via Ki-67 immunohistochemistry and EdU incorporation. Inflammatory and EMT markers (p65-NF-κB, COX-2, vimentin, E-cadherin) will be analyzed by multiplex immunofluorescence. Autophagy flux will be monitored per established guidelines (4th edition) using LC3-II immunoblotting and p62/SQSTM1 degradation in tissue lysates. Data will be quantified using image analysis software (e.g., ImageJ/FIJI with custom macros for tissue segmentation).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence of Calebin A's efficacy in a primary human tissue model, establishing a critical bridge between cell line studies and in vivo physiology. It will delineate how the intact tissue microenvironment modulates TNF-α signaling and therapeutic response, offering a novel experimental platform for evaluating natural compounds in oncology.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Aggarwal's prior work showed Calebin A suppresses TNF-α-induced EMT in colorectal cancer cell lines, this proposal directly tests its activity in the complex, multicellular context of primary human tissue, where stromal-epithelial-immune cell crosstalk is preserved, a significant advance over monolayer cultures.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Fate Mapping of TNF-α-Induced Inflammation and Death: Resolving Heterogeneity in the Pro-Survival and Pro-Death Roles of Autophagy with Calebin A Intervention
proposals.motivation: Tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) is a key inflammatory cytokine in the tumor microenvironment that drives cancer progression, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), and cell death, but cellular responses are highly heterogeneous. Pre-2026, bulk assays mask this heterogeneity, failing to reveal whether TNF-α-induced autophagy in individual cells is a pro-survival mechanism facilitating inflammation-associated tumorigenesis or a pro-death pathway. Resolving this at single-cell resolution is critical for understanding therapy resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TNF-α exposure triggers divergent, stochastic cell fates (survival/EMT vs. death) within an isogenic population, and that the natural compound Calebin A will modulate this fate decision by selectively suppressing pro-survival autophagy and inflammatory signaling in individual cells.
proposals.method: We will use long-term (48-72h) live-cell time-lapse microscopy of colorectal cancer cells (e.g., HCT-116) expressing fluorescent autophagy (LC3-RFP) and death (Annexin V-FITC) reporters following TNF-α exposure ± Calebin A. Individual cell tracking will be performed using the TrackMate plugin in Fiji/ImageJ. Cell fate trajectories (division, EMT-like morphological changes, autophagic flux, death) will be quantified. We will apply Kaplan-Meier-like survival analysis for cell death and correlation algorithms to link early autophagic activity to eventual fate.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, temporal link between TNF-α-induced autophagy dynamics and ultimate cell fate at single-cell resolution. It will delineate the context-dependent role of autophagy in inflammation-driven cancer progression and establish a quantitative framework for evaluating how Calebin A disrupts specific pro-tumorigenic cell trajectories.
proposals.novelty: While bulk studies show Calebin A suppresses TNF-α-induced pathways, this proposal uniquely applies single-cell fate mapping to dissect population heterogeneity, directly testing the dual role of autophagy in cell survival/death decisions within an inflammatory context, which is non-obvious from ensemble averages.

proposals.title: Calebin A Modulates TNF-α-Induced Autophagic Flux to Promote Epithelial Repair in a Chronic Ulcer Model
proposals.motivation: TNF-α is a double-edged cytokine, driving pathological inflammation in chronic diseases like cancer and colitis, yet also essential for tissue repair and homeostasis. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Aggarwal's work, shows Calebin A can suppress TNF-α's tumor-promoting effects in the cancer microenvironment. However, harnessing TNF-α's growth-promoting potential for therapeutic repair in conditions like chronic ulcers remains an underexplored clinical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a low, localized dose of TNF-α can stimulate epithelial cell proliferation and migration for ulcer healing, and that co-administration with Calebin A will fine-tune this response by modulating associated inflammatory signaling and autophagic flux, preventing excessive inflammation and promoting controlled repair.
proposals.method: We will establish an in vitro model of chronic ulceration using TNF-α-induced damage in normal human colonic epithelial cells (FHC line). Cells will be treated with low-dose TNF-α (0.1-1 ng/mL) with/without pre-treatment with Calebin A (5-20 µM). Repair will be assessed via scratch-wound assay, Ki-67 proliferation staining, and transepithelial electrical resistance. Autophagic flux will be quantified per autophagy guidelines using tandem mRFP-GFP-LC3 reporter and immunoblotting for LC3-II/p62. Inflammatory signaling (NF-κB, MAPK) and EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin) will be analyzed to dissect mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel mechanistic framework for repurposing a pro-inflammatory cytokine for regenerative therapy. It will define the role of autophagy in TNF-α-mediated epithelial repair and establish Calebin A as a potential adjuvant to safely harness TNF-α's beneficial effects, bridging the fields of cancer chemoprevention and wound healing.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A is known to suppress TNF-α's tumorigenic effects, its use to modulate TNF-α for therapeutic repair in non-cancerous tissue is novel. The proposal uniquely focuses on leveraging TNF-α's dual nature by targeting autophagy and inflammation to switch its role from destructive to regenerative.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Functional Switching of NF-kappaB in Colorectal Cancer: A Systems-Level Analysis of Tumor-Suppressive Roles and Pharmacological Modulation by Calebin A
proposals.motivation: NF-kappaB signaling is a canonical pro-inflammatory and pro-tumorigenic pathway, a primary target for anti-cancer strategies. However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests its role is not monolithic, with paradoxical tumor-suppressive effects reported in specific contexts, such as in certain genetic backgrounds or during therapy-induced stress. This duality creates a critical knowledge gap, as indiscriminate NF-kappaB inhibition could inadvertently eliminate protective responses. A systematic, context-aware mapping is urgently needed to guide precise therapeutic intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific contextual variables within the tumor ecosystem—including epithelial vs. stromal cell origin, co-existing oncogenic drivers (e.g., KRAS mutation status), and metabolic stress—determine whether NF-kappaB activation exerts net pro-tumorigenic or tumor-suppressive effects, and that the natural compound Calebin A can selectively modulate this context-dependent signaling.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-omics systems biology approach. Using isogenic colorectal cancer (CRC) cell lines with defined genetic backgrounds (e.g., KRAS WT vs. mutant), we will induce specific contextual stresses (hypoxia, chemotherapy, nutrient deprivation) and profile NF-kappaB activity via phospho-proteomics and single-cell RNA-seq. We will integrate this with cytokine arrays to characterize the tumor microenvironment (TME). A Boolean network model, built using tools like CellNOpt, will be trained on this data to predict NF-kappaB functional output (proliferation/apoptosis/EMT) across contexts. Calebin A's effects will be tested in predicted tumor-suppressive vs. pro-tumorigenic contexts, assessing its impact on NF-kappaB subunits (p65 vs. p50) and downstream autophagy (monitored per autophagy guidelines).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first predictive, context-dependent map of NF-kappaB signaling in CRC, moving the field beyond a binary view. It will provide a mechanistic framework to explain contradictory literature findings and identify specific clinical scenarios where NF-kappaB inhibition may be detrimental. Furthermore, it will elucidate a novel, context-selective mechanism of action for Calebin A as a natural product-based therapeutic modulator.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an ecological, systems-level framework to formally model NF-kappaB's context-dependent roles in cancer, a significant conceptual shift from targeting it as a uniformly bad actor. The integration of this modeling with the evaluation of a natural compound (Calebin A) known to suppress inflammation and EMT offers a unique translational bridge to conditional therapy.

proposals.title: A Quantitative Atlas of Pro-Tumorigenic Immune Activity: Defining Its Spectrum and Necessity Across Cancer Etiologies
proposals.motivation: While chronic inflammation is a recognized driver of certain cancers, the extent and necessity of immune activity in cancers not classically linked to inflammation (e.g., certain sarcomas, pediatric cancers) remains poorly defined. This gap limits our understanding of fundamental oncogenic mechanisms and the potential for broad-spectrum immunopreventive strategies. A systematic, quantitative assessment is needed to move beyond the binary classification of cancers as 'inflammatory' or 'non-inflammatory'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a detectable spectrum of pro-tumorigenic immune cell activity and inflammatory mediator signaling is a near-universal feature of early tumor pathogenesis, even in cancers with strong genetic drivers and no known inflammatory etiology, and that this activity is functionally necessary for tumor initiation and progression.
proposals.method: We will employ a tiered platform: 1) Deep immune phenotyping (mass cytometry, cytokine arrays) of genetically engineered mouse models for 'non-inflammatory' cancers (e.g., EWSR1-FLI1-driven Ewing sarcoma). 2) Functional validation using human cancer organoids co-cultured with autologous immune cells, with precise depletion of specific immune subsets (e.g., macrophages via clodronate) or cytokines (via neutralizing antibodies) to assess impact on tumorigenic capacity. 3) Bioinformatic analysis of pre-diagnostic and early-stage tumor samples from immunodeficient patients (e.g., with agammaglobulinemia) for residual inflammatory signatures using RNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first quantitative atlas mapping the intensity and character of pro-tumorigenic immune activity across diverse cancer types. It will establish a new framework for understanding cancer etiology by defining the minimal required immune 'soil' for different genetic 'seeds,' and identify novel, broad targets for chemoprevention, potentially including natural anti-inflammatory agents like Calebin A.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond correlative associations in established tumors to functionally test the necessity of immune activity in the earliest stages of genetically driven, 'non-inflammatory' cancers, a hypothesis not systematically examined pre-2026. The integrated use of immunodeficient patient samples provides a unique human model to test the hypothesis.

proposals.title: Dissecting the Role of Chronic Inflammation in Tumorigenesis: A Necessary and Sufficient Framework Applied to Colorectal and Pediatric Sarcoma Models
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation is a well-established driver of many cancers, particularly colorectal cancer (CRC). However, its role as a universal, obligatory precondition for all cancers remains an assumption. This gap is critical because if inflammation is not necessary, anti-inflammatory chemopreventive strategies, like those involving natural compounds such as Calebin A, may be ineffective for certain cancer types, necessitating a more precise understanding of its etiological role.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic inflammation is necessary and sufficient for inflammation-associated cancers (e.g., colitis-associated CRC) but is neither necessary nor sufficient for tumorigenesis driven primarily by developmental transcription factor aberrations (e.g., Ewing sarcoma).
proposals.method: We will employ a two-pronged genetic strategy. To test NECESSITY, we will use conditional knockout mice lacking key inflammatory mediators (e.g., myeloid-specific IKKβ or IL-6) crossed with established CRC models (ApcMin/+ or oncogenic Kras activation). To test SUFFICIENCY, we will generate mice with inducible, constitutive activation of NF-κB or STAT3 in mesenchymal tissues and monitor for de novo tumor formation. Parallel in vitro studies in isogenic CRC and Ewing sarcoma cell lines will assess the impact of Calebin A on inflammation-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and autophagy, using guidelines from autophagy monitoring assays (4th edition).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide definitive, causal evidence for the scope and limits of the inflammation-cancer paradigm. It will clarify which cancer subtypes are truly inflammation-dependent, thereby refining targets for chemoprevention. Furthermore, it will establish a rigorous experimental framework for testing 'necessary and sufficient' claims in cancer etiology beyond inflammation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly adapting the definitive 'necessary and sufficient' genetic testing paradigm from developmental biology to deconstruct a major cancer hypothesis, moving beyond correlative evidence. This approach is specifically applied to contrast inflammation-driven cancers with those believed to originate from non-inflammatory developmental defects.

proposals.title: Threshold-Based Modulation of NF-kappaB Using Calebin A: An Integrated Management Strategy for the Tumor Microenvironment
proposals.motivation: Chronic NF-kappaB-driven inflammation is a hallmark of cancer progression and therapy resistance, yet its complete suppression can compromise essential immune surveillance and tissue repair. Current therapeutic paradigms often aim for maximal inhibition, potentially mirroring the ecological pitfalls of indiscriminate pest eradication. This project addresses the critical need for a more nuanced, context-dependent strategy to modulate inflammation in oncology, inspired by principles from Integrated Pest Management (IPM).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantifiable threshold of NF-kappaB activity and associated inflammatory mediators exists, above which inflammation becomes tumor-promoting. Calebin A, a natural compound with known anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor properties, can be deployed in a threshold-sensitive manner to suppress pathological inflammation while preserving beneficial immune functions.
proposals.method: Using established colorectal cancer cell models (e.g., HCT-116, SW480) under inflammatory stress (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta), we will first define pathological thresholds by correlating NF-kappaB nuclear translocation dynamics (live-cell imaging with p65-GFP), cytokine secretion profiles (multiplex ELISA), and functional outcomes (epithelial-mesenchymal transition markers, cell invasion). We will then establish an intervention protocol where Calebin A treatment is initiated only when predefined biomarker thresholds (e.g., sustained p65 nuclear localization >2 hours, IL-6 >500 pg/ml) are breached. Efficacy will be assessed by comparing continuous vs. threshold-triggered Calebin A dosing on tumor spheroid growth, autophagy flux (LC3-II/p62 immunoblotting per established guidelines), and chemosensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel conceptual framework for 'adaptive anti-inflammatory therapy' in cancer, moving beyond blanket suppression. It will deliver specific, measurable biomarker thresholds to guide intervention and demonstrate the therapeutic advantage of context-aware dosing of a natural agent like Calebin A. The findings could redefine how inflammation is managed in the tumor microenvironment to improve treatment outcomes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translational of ecological IPM principles into cancer immunology to develop a threshold-based therapeutic strategy, a paradigm distinct from conventional continuous inhibition. Furthermore, applying this concept using the multi-target agent Calebin A, which modulates inflammation and autophagy, represents a unique, systems-level approach.

proposals.title: Stage-Specific Inflammatory Programs Govern Tumor Progression: A Spatiotemporal Map for Targeted Immunomodulation in Breast and Pancreatic Cancer
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation is a recognized hallmark of cancer, but current therapeutic strategies targeting it, such as broad anti-inflammatory agents, have shown limited success and can be immunosuppressive. This failure suggests inflammation is not a monolithic driver but a dynamic, context-dependent process. A precise, stage-resolved understanding of which specific inflammatory cell states and signaling modules are pro- or anti-tumorigenic at distinct phases—from initiation to metastasis—is critically needed to develop effective, stage-targeted therapies without compromising host defense.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tumor progression is driven by discrete, stage-specific inflammatory programs orchestrated by unique myeloid cell populations and cytokine networks, and that genetic ablation of these context-dependent modules will selectively inhibit progression at defined transition points.
proposals.method: We will use inducible GEMMs for breast (MMTV-PyMT) and pancreatic (KPC) cancer, performing longitudinal sampling. Tumor and microenvironment will be analyzed using 10x Genomics single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) and Visium spatial transcriptomics at key stages: pre-neoplasia, early carcinoma, invasive carcinoma, and metastatic lesions. Data integration and cell-state definition will use Seurat and Monocle3 for trajectory analysis. Identified stage-specific modules (e.g., a unique tumor-associated macrophage subset or IL-1β/IL-6 signaling hub) will be causally tested using Cre-loxP systems (e.g., LysM-Cre or Csf1r-iCre) for myeloid ablation or cytokine receptor knockout at specific timepoints.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution spatiotemporal atlas of inflammatory states across two aggressive carcinomas, moving beyond static 'snapshots'. It will causally link specific inflammatory modules to discrete progression steps, identifying precise therapeutic windows. The findings will provide a rationale for stage-specific immunomodulation, potentially using natural agents like Calebin A, which has shown anti-inflammatory and EMT-suppressive activity in pre-2026 studies, to target pro-tumorigenic programs without systemic immunosuppression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links inflammation to cancer broadly, this project uniquely integrates longitudinal single-cell and spatial genomics with stage-specific genetic perturbations to deconvolute the dynamic roles of inflammation, offering a mechanistic, phase-specific roadmap for intervention rather than a blanket approach.

proposals.title: Calebin A Targets Inflammation as a Critical Driver of Secondary Succession in the Metastatic Niche: A Pre-Clinical Investigation
proposals.motivation: Metastasis is the primary cause of cancer mortality, and the pre-metastatic niche, primed by primary tumor-derived factors, is a critical determinant of organotropism and colonization success. While inflammation is a known hallmark of cancer, its specific and differential role in the distinct stages of metastasis—initial dissemination versus colonization of a conditioned niche—remains poorly defined. This gap limits the development of stage-specific anti-metastatic therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inflammatory pathways, particularly NF-κB, function analogously to facilitators of ecological secondary succession, being more critical for tumor cell colonization within a pre-conditioned, inflammatory pre-metastatic niche than for initial seeding into a naive organ microenvironment.
proposals.method: We will use a syngeneic mouse model of colorectal cancer (CT26 cells) to establish a pre-metastatic niche in the liver via primary tumor-derived factors (e.g., extracellular vesicles). We will then compare metastatic burden following tumor cell injection into naive mice versus mice with an established pre-metastatic niche. In both groups, we will pharmacologically inhibit inflammatory pathways using the natural compound Calebin A (a known NF-κB and inflammation suppressor from Dr. Aggarwal's prior work) and assess key metrics: tumor cell seeding (via bioluminescence), outgrowth (histology), and molecular markers of EMT, autophagy, and niche-associated inflammation (cytokine profiling, IHC, western blot).
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the stage-specific importance of inflammation in metastasis, providing a mechanistic framework for the 'secondary succession' analogy. It will directly evaluate the therapeutic potential of Calebin A as a niche-disrupting, anti-metastatic agent that targets the facilitated colonization phase, moving beyond its established anti-tumorigenic roles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying an ecological succession framework to experimentally partition the metastatic process, challenging the assumption of inflammation's uniform role. It uniquely positions Calebin A not just as a general anti-inflammatory anti-cancer agent, but as a specific inhibitor of the inflammatory facilitation required for colonization within a pre-conditioned niche.

proposals.title: Precision Targeting of Context-Specific NF-kappaB Network Nodes in Colitis-Associated Cancer Using Multi-Omic Profiling and Natural Compound Intervention
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation, driven by NF-kappaB, is a major initiator of cancers like colorectal cancer (CRC). However, systemic NF-kappaB inhibition disrupts its essential physiological roles in immunity and tissue homeostasis, leading to toxicity. A precision strategy to selectively inhibit pathogenic NF-kappaB signaling in pre-malignant lesions, while sparing its protective functions, is critically needed for effective cancer interception with minimal side effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multi-omic profiling of inflammation-induced epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in pre-malignant colonic epithelium will reveal specific, actionable NF-kappaB network nodes (e.g., unique upstream kinases or co-activator complexes) whose targeted perturbation by agents like Calebin A can suppress tumor-promoting signaling without broadly suppressing canonical NF-kappaB activity.
proposals.method: We will use an established in vitro model of TNF-alpha-induced, inflammation-driven EMT in human colorectal cancer cells (e.g., HCT116). Multi-omic profiling (phospho-proteomics, RNA-seq, and targeted metabolomics) will map the specific NF-kappaB network architecture activated in this context. Computational network analysis (using tools like Cytoscape with ReactomeFI) will identify key driver nodes. We will then test the natural compound Calebin A, known to suppress inflammation-induced EMT, for its ability to selectively inhibit these identified driver nodes (e.g., specific IKK phosphorylation events or p65/co-activator interactions) using co-immunoprecipitation, kinase activity assays, and autophagy flux monitoring (per autophagy guidelines 4th edition), while assessing global NF-kappaB activity via reporter assays and cytokine arrays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a blueprint for moving beyond blanket NF-kappaB inhibition to context-specific network perturbation. It will deliver a validated multi-omic signature of 'actionable' pathogenic NF-kappaB signaling in pre-malignancy and demonstrate the mechanistic precision of a natural compound (Calebin A) as a proof-of-concept targeted agent. This contributes directly to the development of biomarker-driven interception strategies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating multi-omic network analysis of a pre-malignant, inflammation-induced state to identify and then pharmacologically validate specific NF-kappaB network nodes for precision targeting, rather than testing generic NF-kappaB inhibitors. This shifts the paradigm from pathway blockade to network perturbation.

proposals.title: Synthetic Dissection of Viral Oncoprotein-Induced NF-kB Dynamics and Their Causal Role in Inflammation-Driven Transformation
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation, often driven by persistent viral infections like HPV and HCV, is a well-established tumor promoter, with NF-kB being a central mediator. However, it remains unclear whether viral protein-induced NF-kB activation is a passenger inflammatory response or a direct driver of oncogenic transformation. Establishing this causality is critical for developing targeted anti-cancer strategies that specifically disrupt pro-tumorigenic signaling without affecting host defense.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific spatiotemporal dynamics of NF-kB activation, induced by viral oncoproteins, are sufficient to drive epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and transformation, and that these dynamics depend on a distinct set of host interactors that can be therapeutically targeted.
proposals.method: We will engineer isogenic cell lines with optogenetically or chemically inducible NF-kB modules to precisely control signaling amplitude and duration, testing sufficiency for transformation phenotypes. In parallel, we will conduct CRISPR/Cas9 knockout screens in cells expressing HIV Tat, HPV E6/E7, or HCV core proteins, using a NF-kB reporter (e.g., secreted luciferase) and EMT markers (e.g., E-cadherin loss) as phenotypic readouts to identify necessary host factors. Bioinformatic analysis will integrate screen hits with known inflammatory and autophagy pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct causal link between specific NF-kB signaling dynamics induced by viral proteins and oncogenic transformation, moving beyond correlation. It will identify novel, targetable host dependencies for viral-associated cancers and provide a functional genomics framework to distinguish driver from passenger inflammatory events in oncogenesis.
proposals.novelty: The integration of synthetic biology to test the sufficiency of specific NF-kB dynamics with functional genomics to identify necessary host interactors for viral proteins provides a uniquely powerful, causal, and mechanistic approach, distinct from prior correlative studies of inflammation and cancer.

proposals.title: A Dual-Sensor Nanoparticle for Tumor-Microenvironment-Activated Delivery of Calebin A to Suppress Therapy-Induced NF-κB and Overcome Chemoresistance in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Chemotherapy induces a pro-inflammatory, pro-survival tumor microenvironment (TME) via NF-κB activation, driving epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and therapy resistance. Systemic NF-κB inhibition is toxic and impairs beneficial immune responses. There is a critical unmet need for strategies to precisely suppress therapy-induced inflammation within tumors without systemic immunosuppression.
proposals.hypothesis: A nanoparticle that co-delivers a chemotherapeutic agent (e.g., 5-FU) and releases the natural NF-κB inhibitor Calebin A only in response to dual TME signals (chemotherapy metabolite and MMP-9 protease) will locally quench therapy-induced pro-survival signaling, suppress EMT, and re-sensitize colorectal cancer cells, while preserving systemic immunity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a lipid-polymer hybrid nanoparticle encapsulating 5-FU and a protease-cleavable Calebin A prodrug. The nanoparticle surface will be grafted with a metabolite-responsive polymer shell (e.g., reactive oxygen species (ROS)-cleavable linkers) that degrades upon exposure to chemotherapy-induced metabolic stress. Subsequent exposure to TME protease MMP-9 will cleave the prodrug, releasing active Calebin A. We will validate activation kinetics in vitro using conditioned media from treated colorectal cancer cells (HCT-116, SW480) and assess functional outcomes via assays for NF-κB activity (luciferase reporter, p65 nuclear translocation), EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin), autophagy (LC3-II flux per guidelines), and chemosensitivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, spatially targeted strategy to disrupt the link between therapy-induced inflammation and resistance, moving beyond blunt NF-κB inhibition. It will establish proof-of-concept for a 'therapy-activated' anti-inflammatory delivery system and elucidate the role of localized Calebin A in suppressing EMT and autophagy-associated survival pathways in the stressed TME.
proposals.novelty: The dual-sensor (metabolite + protease) activation logic ensures payload release specifically in therapy-stressed tumor tissue, a significant advance over single-stimuli systems. Integrating the known anti-inflammatory and EMT-suppressive properties of Calebin A into a context-activated delivery platform for chemosensitization is a novel combinatorial approach.

proposals.title: Calebin A as an NF-κB inhibitor to suppress therapy-induced inflammation and overcome chemoresistance in colorectal cancer models
proposals.motivation: Conventional chemotherapy and radiation often activate NF-κB, a master regulator of inflammation, leading to a pro-survival tumor microenvironment, induction of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), and acquired therapy resistance. This inflammation-driven feedback loop significantly limits treatment efficacy in cancers like colorectal carcinoma (CRC). Pre-2026 evidence suggests targeting this pathway is a promising strategy to improve therapeutic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining the natural NF-κB inhibitor Calebin A with standard chemotherapeutic agents (e.g., 5-FU, oxaliplatin) will suppress therapy-induced inflammation and EMT, thereby sensitizing colorectal cancer cells to treatment and overcoming acquired chemoresistance.
proposals.method: We will use established human CRC cell lines (HCT-116, SW480) and a chemoresistant derivative line. Cells will be treated with chemotherapeutics alone or in combination with Calebin A. NF-κB activation (nuclear translocation of p65), inflammatory cytokine secretion (IL-6, TNF-α via ELISA), and EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin, Snail via western blot) will be quantified. Autophagic flux will be assessed per established guidelines (LC3-II turnover, p62 degradation). Synergy will be evaluated using Chou-Talalay combination index, and in vivo validation will be performed in a xenograft mouse model measuring tumor volume, metastasis, and immunohistochemical analysis of the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide mechanistic evidence on how Calebin A disrupts the inflammation-EMT-resistance axis triggered by chemotherapy. It will establish a preclinical rationale for using this natural compound as an adjuvant to enhance the efficacy of standard CRC treatments and mitigate adverse pro-tumorigenic side effects.
proposals.novelty: While NF-κB inhibition is a known concept, the specific application of Calebin A to counteract the tumor-promoting inflammation *induced by chemotherapy itself* in CRC, and its link to modulating both EMT and autophagy within this context, represents a novel and targeted therapeutic strategy building directly on Dr. Aggarwal's prior findings.

proposals.title: Integrated High-Throughput Screening for NF-κB Inhibitors with Enhanced Chronic-Use Safety: A Focus on Calebin A and Curcuminoid Derivatives
proposals.motivation: Chronic, low-grade inflammation driven by NF-κB is a hallmark of cancer and other chronic diseases. While natural products like curcumin and calebin A show potent anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor activity, their clinical translation is hampered by poor bioavailability and, critically, undefined long-term safety profiles for high-dose, chronic supplementation. Proactively identifying and mitigating off-target toxicities, such as cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzyme inhibition or induction of detrimental cellular stress, is a major unmet need in natural product-based drug discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic derivatives of calebin A and related curcuminoids can be engineered through structure-activity relationship (SAR)-driven design to maintain potent NF-κB inhibitory and anti-tumor efficacy while exhibiting a significantly reduced potential for mechanism-based long-term toxicity compared to their parent compounds.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-readout, high-throughput screening (HTS) platform. A library of ~5,000 synthetic derivatives will be screened in parallel using (1) a primary NF-κB luciferase reporter assay in TNF-α-induced inflammatory models (e.g., colorectal cancer cells) to assess potency, and (2) secondary counter-screens measuring off-target effects. These include fluorescence-based assays for inhibition of major human CYP isoforms (3A4, 2D6) and a high-content imaging assay quantifying markers of excessive/unresolved autophagy (LC3-II/p62) and integrated stress response (ATF4, CHOP) activation. Hits from the primary screen will be ranked by a proprietary selectivity score balancing NF-κB IC50 against minimal off-target activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield novel, safer lead candidates targeting NF-κB for chronic inflammatory diseases and cancer prevention. It will establish a new screening paradigm that integrates early toxicity profiling directly into the discovery pipeline for natural product derivatives. The data will provide a crucial SAR map linking chemical modifications to both efficacy and safety endpoints.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent, integrated screening for efficacy and chronic-use safety mechanisms at the initial HTS stage, a significant departure from the sequential approach where safety is assessed much later. This proactively addresses the hidden risks of long-term, high-dose nutraceutical use by design.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening for Novel NF-κB Inhibitors: Targeting Inflammation-Driven Tumor Microenvironment and EMT in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: The NF-κB pathway is a master regulator of inflammation, tumor promotion, and therapy resistance, particularly in colorectal cancer (CRC). Persistent NF-κB activation in the tumor microenvironment drives epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), metastasis, and suppresses autophagic tumor suppression. Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Aggarwal's work on Calebin A, highlights NF-κB as a critical target, but more potent and specific small-molecule inhibitors are needed for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput chemical library screen will identify novel, structurally distinct small molecules that inhibit NF-κB activation with greater potency and specificity than current natural compounds, thereby suppressing inflammation-induced EMT and tumor progression in CRC models.
proposals.method: We will employ a cell-based high-throughput screening (HTS) platform using a CRC cell line (e.g., HCT-116) stably transfected with an NF-κB-responsive luciferase reporter. A library of ~100,000 synthetic and natural compound derivatives will be screened for inhibition of TNF-α-induced NF-κB activation. Primary hits will be validated in secondary assays measuring IκBα degradation (western blot), p65 nuclear translocation (immunofluorescence), and downstream pro-inflammatory gene expression (qPCR). Positive compounds will be prioritized based on IC50 and tested for their ability to reverse TGF-β/TNF-α-induced EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin) and modulate autophagy (LC3-II flux).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new pipeline of lead NF-κB inhibitor candidates for pre-clinical development in inflammation-driven cancers. It will mechanistically link NF-κB inhibition to the suppression of EMT and modulation of autophagy in the CRC microenvironment, providing a multi-targeted therapeutic strategy. The findings will expand the pharmacological toolkit beyond curcumin analogs like Calebin A.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A is a known natural NF-κB inhibitor, this proposal systematically applies modern HTS from synthetic drug discovery to find novel chemotypes with potentially superior drug-like properties. It uniquely integrates NF-κB inhibition readouts with functional EMT and autophagy assays early in the screening cascade, focusing on microenvironmental drivers of CRC progression.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Meta-Analysis to Quantify the Prevalence of Constitutive NF-κB Activation and Its Association with Tumor Microenvironment Inflammation
proposals.motivation: Constitutive activation of the NF-κB pathway is a known driver of chronic inflammation, tumor progression, and therapy resistance across numerous cancers. However, pre-2026 evidence is fragmented, with prevalence estimates varying widely by study and cancer type, hindering the assessment of NF-κB as a universal therapeutic target. A systematic, quantitative synthesis is urgently needed to establish a definitive baseline and identify cancers where NF-κB inhibition, potentially by agents like Calebin A, could have the broadest impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that constitutive NF-κB activation is a prevalent hallmark across most solid and hematological malignancies, and its prevalence positively correlates with established markers of an inflammatory tumor microenvironment and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT).
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic review and meta-analysis following PRISMA guidelines. A comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, and Web of Science (up to Dec 2025) will identify studies reporting immunohistochemical, electrophoretic mobility shift assay (EMSA), or nuclear translocation data for NF-κB (p65/p50) in human cancer tissues versus normal controls. Data on prevalence, cancer type, stage, and associated markers (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, vimentin) will be extracted. Pooled prevalence estimates with 95% confidence intervals will be calculated for each cancer type using a random-effects model (DerSimonian and Laird method) in R (metafor package). Subgroup and meta-regression analyses will assess associations with inflammatory and EMT markers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, pan-cancer estimate of constitutive NF-κB activation prevalence, creating a definitive reference for the field. It will identify cancer types with the highest dependency on this pathway, thereby prioritizing targets for anti-inflammatory therapies like Calebin A. The analysis will also formally establish the statistical link between NF-κB activation and key tumor microenvironment features, strengthening the rationale for combination strategies targeting inflammation and autophagy.
proposals.novelty: While NF-κB's role is well-studied in specific cancers, a formal meta-analysis to quantify its pan-cancer prevalence and correlate it with microenvironmental inflammation has not been performed. This study uniquely bridges Dr. Aggarwal's expertise in Calebin A, inflammation, and tumor microenvironment by providing the epidemiological foundation to strategically apply such NF-κB inhibitors across cancer types.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Investigation of Calebin A in Disrupting Tumor-Induced Inflammatory Amplification Loops in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research established that chronic inflammation is a hallmark of cancer, but the reciprocal process—how established tumors actively reprogram and amplify their inflammatory microenvironment to fuel progression—is less understood. This 'inverse problem' is critical as tumor-sustained inflammation drives immunosuppression, therapy resistance, and metastasis. Addressing this gap can reveal novel therapeutic targets to break this pro-tumorigenic cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that established colorectal cancer (CRC) cells secrete specific factors that induce a pro-tumorigenic, amplified inflammatory response in tumor-associated macrophages (TAMs) and fibroblasts, and that the natural compound Calebin A can suppress this tumor-initiated inflammatory reprogramming by targeting key signaling nodes like NF-κB and STAT3.
proposals.method: We will use a co-culture system of patient-derived CRC spheroids with primary human macrophages/fibroblasts to model the tumor microenvironment (TME). Conditioned media from CRC cells will be analyzed via cytokine arrays to identify key inflammatory amplifiers (e.g., IL-6, IL-8, MCP-1). The role of autophagy in this secretory phenotype will be assessed using LC3-II/p62 immunoblotting per autophagy guidelines. We will treat co-cultures with Calebin A and measure downstream inflammatory markers (NF-κB/p65 nuclear translocation, phospho-STAT3, COX-2, TNF-α) via immunofluorescence and ELISA. Functional readouts will include TAM polarization (CD206/CD86 flow cytometry) and invasion assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will mechanistically delineate how tumors actively sustain a pro-inflammatory TME, moving beyond the paradigm of inflammation merely initiating cancer. It will position Calebin A as a multi-target agent capable of disrupting tumor-induced inflammatory circuits, providing a preclinical rationale for its use as an adjunct therapy to mitigate CRC progression and therapy resistance.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A is known for general anti-inflammatory effects, its specific capacity to intercept and suppress inflammation *orchestrated by the tumor itself*, rather than host-derived inflammation, is a novel and targeted therapeutic concept. The study uniquely focuses on the tumor as the instigator of microenvironmental reprogramming.

proposals.title: Calebin A Attenuates Neuroinflammation and Modulates Autophagy to Protect Against Amyloid-β-Induced Neuronal Toxicity in Alzheimer's Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Chronic neuroinflammation, driven by persistent NF-κB activation, is a central pathological feature in Alzheimer's disease (AD) and contributes to neuronal loss and cognitive decline. Pre-2026 evidence shows Calebin A, a curcumin analog, potently inhibits NF-κB and inflammatory pathways in cancer models, but its therapeutic potential in non-cancer, inflammation-driven neurodegenerative diseases remains unexplored. This represents a significant translational gap, as safe NF-κB modulators are urgently needed for AD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Calebin A will suppress amyloid-β-induced neuroinflammation by inhibiting the NF-κB pathway and will concomitantly modulate associated autophagic flux, leading to reduced neuronal apoptosis and improved neuronal survival in cellular and invertebrate models of Alzheimer's disease.
proposals.method: We will employ established pre-2026 cellular and invertebrate AD models. SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells will be treated with oligomeric Aβ1-42 to induce toxicity and inflammation. Using specific inhibitors and GFP-LC3 transfection assays per established autophagy guidelines, we will quantify autophagic flux via immunoblotting for LC3-II/p62 and confocal microscopy. NF-κB activation (p65 nuclear translocation), pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α via ELISA), and apoptosis (caspase-3 cleavage, TUNEL assay) will be assessed. Validation will be performed in a transgenic C. elegans AD model (CL4176) by analyzing paralysis rates and neuronal staining.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct evidence for the neuroprotective efficacy of Calebin A against Aβ-driven pathology, specifically linking its anti-inflammatory action to autophagy modulation. It will establish a novel pre-clinical rationale for repurposing this NF-κB-targeting agent for Alzheimer's disease, expanding its therapeutic scope beyond oncology.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A's anti-NF-κB role in cancer is known, its application to Aβ-induced neuroinflammation and its specific impact on neuronal autophagy in AD models is entirely novel, moving the compound into the neurodegeneration field.

proposals.title: Viral Proteins as Keystone Perturbants: Mapping Convergent Host Interactomes for NF-kappaB Activation in Viral Carcinogenesis
proposals.motivation: Chronic viral infections (e.g., HIV, HPV, HCV) are major cancer drivers, frequently hijacking the pro-inflammatory and pro-survival NF-kappaB pathway. However, the precise mechanisms are often unclear, as viral proteins can have pleiotropic effects. A systematic, network-level understanding of how distinct viruses converge on NF-kappaB activation is lacking, limiting targeted intervention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that viral oncoproteins from HIV, HPV, and HCV act as 'keystone perturbants' within the host protein interaction network, and that mapping their distinct interactomes will reveal convergent, targetable host nodes essential for NF-kappaB activation and subsequent tumor promotion.
proposals.method: We will employ affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS) to comprehensively map the host protein interactomes of key viral proteins (e.g., HIV Tat, HPV E6, HCV core). Functional validation will use siRNA-based high-throughput screening against identified host nodes, measuring NF-kappaB reporter activity and inflammatory cytokine output. The resulting data will be integrated into a unified network model using Cytoscape, with topological analysis (betweenness centrality, clustering coefficients) to identify critical convergent host proteins. The role of these hubs in viral-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and autophagy will be assessed.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comparative, systems-level map of host interactomes hijacked by major viral carcinogens to activate NF-kappaB. It will identify novel host-centric therapeutic targets that could disrupt oncogenic signaling across multiple virus types. The findings will advance the conceptual framework of viral carcinogenesis from a linear pathway view to a network perturbation model.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting the ecological keystone species theory to virology, treating viral proteins as network perturbants, and using comparative interactomics to distinguish direct triggers from indirect network effects in NF-kappaB activation, a departure from studying single viral-host protein pairs.

proposals.title: A Tripartite Strategy to Block Therapy-Induced Tumor Microenvironment Crosstalk: Combining Cytotoxic Therapy, NF-κB Inhibition, and Calebin A-Mediated Disruption of Adhesion and Paracrine Signaling in Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Conventional cancer therapies like chemotherapy and radiation often induce a pro-inflammatory and pro-survival tumor microenvironment (TME), characterized by NF-κB activation, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT), and enhanced stromal crosstalk, which ultimately limits treatment efficacy and promotes relapse. This therapy-induced 'microenvironmental biofilm' is a critical but inadequately addressed driver of therapeutic resistance. Pre-2026 work by Aggarwal and others has established the role of inflammation and specific agents like Calebin A in modulating these pathways, yet a comprehensive strategy to actively prevent the establishment of this resistant niche is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combining a standard cytotoxic agent (5-FU/oxaliplatin) with an NF-κB inhibitor (e.g., BAY 11-7082) and the natural multi-target agent Calebin A will synergistically enhance therapy durability by not only killing tumor cells and dampening inflammation but also directly disrupting the therapy-induced adhesion and paracrine signaling events between colorectal cancer cells and cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) that are essential for creating a pro-tumorigenic niche.
proposals.method: We will use established 2D and 3D co-culture models of human colorectal cancer cells (HCT-116, SW480) and primary CAFs. Cells will be treated with 5-FU/radiation ± NF-κB inhibitor ± Calebin A. We will quantify therapy-induced changes in: 1) NF-κB activation (p65 nuclear translocation via immunofluorescence), 2) EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin, Snail via western blot), 3) cell adhesion (integrin β1/FAK signaling and spheroid cohesion assays), and 4) paracrine signaling (IL-6, TGF-β secretion via ELISA). Autophagic flux will be monitored per established guidelines (LC3-II/p62). Synergy will be assessed via Chou-Talalay combination index.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistically grounded combinatorial strategy to overcome therapy-induced microenvironmental resistance. It will directly test the 'antifouling' analogy in oncology, offering a new conceptual framework for treatment design. The findings will specifically advance the translational potential of Calebin A by defining its role in disrupting TME crosstalk beyond its known anti-inflammatory and anti-EMT effects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual adaptation of an antifouling strategy to actively 'coat' and disrupt the forming pro-tumor niche, and in the specific tripartite combination that adds a TME-disrupting agent (Calebin A) to the conventional duo of cytotoxic and anti-inflammatory drugs, targeting the root cause of resistance rather than its consequences.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Cancer Risk Inflammatory Signature (CRIS) Score: A Multi-Marker Panel for Risk Stratification Inspired by Cardiovascular Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation is a well-established enabling characteristic of cancer, driving tumor initiation and progression, yet it remains underutilized for population-level risk assessment. In cardiovascular disease (CVD), validated multi-marker panels integrating inflammatory markers like hs-CRP have significantly improved risk prediction and prevention strategies. This project aims to translate this successful paradigm to oncology by defining a systemic inflammatory signature to identify high-risk individuals for targeted screening and intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: A composite score derived from a core panel of specific circulating inflammatory mediators, combined with key clinical risk factors, can stratify individuals by their risk of developing cancer, analogous to validated CVD risk scores.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a retrospective case-control study using pre-diagnostic serum/plasma samples from biobanks (e.g., PLCO, UK Biobank). A multiplex cytokine/chemokine panel (e.g., IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8, IL-10) and acute-phase proteins (e.g., CRP, SAA) will be measured. Clinical data (age, BMI, smoking, family history) will be integrated. Using machine learning (LASSO regression for feature selection, followed by logistic regression or a Cox proportional-hazards model), we will develop the CRIS algorithm. Validation will be performed in an independent cohort.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, quantitative tool for cancer risk stratification, moving beyond single biomarkers. It will provide a framework for personalized screening protocols and create a baseline metric to evaluate the efficacy of preventive anti-inflammatory interventions, such as dietary agents like curcumin or calebin A, in high-risk populations.
proposals.novelty: While inflammation is known in cancer, the systematic adaptation of the multi-marker, composite risk score model from cardiology to create a validated cancer risk algorithm is novel. It directly leverages Dr. Aggarwal's expertise in linking inflammation to cancer and evaluating anti-inflammatory phytochemicals within a new predictive clinical context.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Phenotypic Screening for NF-κB Pathway Inhibitors Using Disease-Relevant Inflammatory Outputs
proposals.motivation: Chronic, NF-κB-driven inflammation is a critical mediator in tumor progression, therapy resistance, and metastasis, as evidenced in colorectal cancer models. Current screens often rely on simplified reporter assays, which may miss compounds that modulate specific pathological outputs or act in complex cellular contexts like the tumor microenvironment. A more physiologically relevant discovery strategy is needed to identify novel agents that effectively disrupt the disease-promoting functions of NF-κB.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput phenotypic screen measuring integrated inflammatory outputs—rather than just pathway activation—in relevant human cell models will identify novel small-molecule NF-κB inhibitors with distinct mechanisms and superior efficacy in suppressing disease-associated phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-content phenotypic screen using TNF-α-stimulated human THP-1 macrophages and HUVEC endothelial cells. A diverse library of small molecules will be applied, and the primary readout will be the high-throughput measurement of a panel of key NF-κB-dependent outputs: secreted cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α via multiplex ELISA) and cell surface adhesion molecule (ICAM-1, VCAM-1) expression via automated immunofluorescence imaging. Hit compounds will be validated in secondary assays for NF-κB nuclear translocation (imaging), IκBα degradation (western blot), and their ability to suppress inflammation-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition in co-culture models of colorectal cancer.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel set of candidate NF-κB inhibitors validated for their ability to block integrated inflammatory responses. It will provide a validated phenotypic screening platform for inflammation-associated drug discovery and may yield lead compounds with potential application in cancer prevention and therapy, particularly for inflammation-driven cancers.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel by directly adapting the 'whole-system' phenotypic screening philosophy from agrochemical discovery to inflammatory disease, moving beyond canonical reporter assays to screen for functional modulation of disease-relevant cellular phenotypes in human cells.

proposals.title: Comparative Functional and Immunogenic Analysis of Recombinant vs. Native TNF-α: Implications for Inflammatory Signaling and Tumor Microenvironment Modulation
proposals.motivation: Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF-α) is a pleiotropic cytokine central to inflammation-associated tumorigenesis and cancer progression. While recombinant human TNF-α (rhTNF-α) produced in E. coli is widely used in research, its lack of mammalian post-translational modifications (PTMs) may alter its functional profile and receptor interactions compared to native, monocyte-derived TNF-α. Understanding these differences is critical for interpreting studies on TNF-α's role in inflammation-induced epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and tumor microenvironment dynamics, areas of direct relevance to Dr. Aggarwal's work on Calebin A.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that E. coli-produced recombinant TNF-α, while bioactive, exhibits altered functional kinetics and immunogenic properties compared to native human TNF-α due to the absence of specific PTMs, leading to differential activation of NF-κB, inflammatory signaling, and EMT-related pathways in colorectal cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will produce and purify rhTNF-α from E. coli and isolate native TNF-α from stimulated human monocytes. Using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), we will map and compare their PTM profiles. Functional activity will be assessed via cytotoxicity assays (L929 cells) and NF-κB activation (luciferase reporter). In colorectal cancer cell lines (HCT-116, SW480), we will compare the cytokines' ability to induce EMT markers (vimentin, N-cadherin, Snail) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8) via qPCR and immunoblotting, and test Calebin A's efficacy in suppressing pathways activated by each TNF-α form.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive, side-by-side biochemical and functional characterization of recombinant versus native TNF-α, clarifying a key variable in inflammation and cancer research. It will directly inform the interpretation of prior and future studies using recombinant TNF, particularly in the context of the tumor microenvironment and EMT. The findings will enhance the mechanistic understanding of how natural compounds like Calebin A counteract TNF-driven oncogenic pathways.
proposals.novelty: While recombinant TNF is assumed to be fully active, a systematic pre-2026 comparison of its PTM-deficient structure with native TNF, specifically evaluating consequent differences in EMT induction and modulatory response to a natural anti-inflammatory agent (Calebin A), has not been comprehensively reported.

proposals.title: Calebin A as a TNF-modulating adjuvant to enhance dendritic cell maturation and antigen-specific T-cell responses in a model cancer vaccine
proposals.motivation: Tumor Necrosis Factor-alpha (TNF) is a potent immune modulator with known adjuvant properties, but its systemic toxicity limits therapeutic use. Pre-2026 research by Dr. Aggarwal has shown that the natural compound Calebin A can suppress TNF-induced pro-inflammatory pathways in cancer cells. This creates a unique opportunity to explore whether Calebin A can be used to 'tune' TNF's adjuvant activity, potentially mitigating toxicity while preserving or enhancing its immune-stimulating effects for vaccine development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-administration of Calebin A with low-dose TNF will modulate the inflammatory tumor microenvironment, leading to enhanced dendritic cell (DC) maturation, antigen cross-presentation, and a durable, antigen-specific cytotoxic T-lymphocyte (CTL) response when used as part of a peptide-based cancer vaccine.
proposals.method: We will use a pre-clinical murine model of colorectal cancer (CT26 cells). Bone marrow-derived dendritic cells (BMDCs) will be treated in vitro with a tumor-associated antigen (e.g., AH1 peptide), low-dose recombinant murine TNF, and varying concentrations of Calebin A. DC maturation (CD80, CD86, MHC-II) and cytokine secretion (IL-12, IL-6) will be assessed by flow cytometry and ELISA. For in vivo validation, mice will receive a therapeutic vaccine comprising AH1 peptide, low-dose TNF, and Calebin A. Tumor growth, tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (CD8+, IFN-γ+), and markers of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) in the tumor will be analyzed, linking adjuvant effect to suppression of the TNF-induced immunosuppressive microenvironment.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel pharmacological strategy to harness TNF's adjuvant potential safely. It will establish proof-of-concept for using natural compounds like Calebin A as 'adjuvant modulators' in cancer vaccines. The findings could translate to a new class of combination adjuvants that enhance anti-tumor immunity while counteracting the tumor-promoting inflammation associated with chronic TNF exposure.
proposals.novelty: While TNF is known as an adjuvant and Calebin A as a TNF inhibitor in cancer cells, their combined use to selectively shape the immune response for vaccination is unexplored. This approach moves beyond using TNF for direct tumor killing or Calebin A solely for chemoprevention, instead repurposing them as a synergistic pair for immune potentiation.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Chronic Toxicity and Pro-Tumorigenic Paradox in Calebin A: Integrating Autophagy Modulation, Inflammation, and Tumor Microenvironment Crosstalk
proposals.motivation: Natural compounds like Calebin A are promising for chronic chemoprevention due to perceived safety, but long-term, high-dose use requires rigorous safety assessment. Pre-2026 evidence shows Calebin A modulates key pathways like inflammation and autophagy, which are double-edged swords; chronic inhibition or over-activation can potentially promote tumorigenesis or cause organ toxicity. A systematic study is needed to evaluate if sustained exposure to Calebin A, while suppressing acute inflammation, inadvertently creates a pro-tumorigenic niche or induces adverse cellular adaptations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that chronic, high-dose administration of Calebin A, despite its acute anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor effects, may induce compensatory pro-survival autophagy, alter the tumor microenvironment, and lead to measurable organ toxicity or paradoxical pro-tumorigenic signaling in vivo.
proposals.method: We will employ a 6-month chronic dosing study in a colitis-associated colorectal cancer (CAC) mouse model, administering three doses of Calebin A. Toxicity will be assessed via serum biochemistry, histopathology of liver/kidney, and hematological analysis. Tumor burden and multiplicity will be quantified. Mechanistically, we will analyze tumor tissues using IHC and immunoblotting for EMT markers (E-cadherin, vimentin), autophagy flux (LC3-II/p62 via guidelines from autophagy assays paper), and inflammatory cytokines (NF-κB, IL-6). RNA-seq will identify compensatory pathways activated in the tumor microenvironment after long-term exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive preclinical safety profile for chronic Calebin A use, directly addressing the 'inherently safe' paradigm. It will delineate the dose-response window between efficacy and toxicity and reveal potential long-term risks related to autophagy and microenvironment adaptation, offering critical data for translational development.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on Calebin A's acute anti-tumor effects, this proposal uniquely investigates the long-term consequences and potential paradoxical effects, integrating chronic toxicity with detailed analysis of autophagy flux and EMT in the tumor microenvironment—a critical gap in natural compound research.

proposals.title: Calebin A Modulates Nrf2/ARE and NF-κB Signaling to Counteract Inflammation-Associated Oxidative Stress in a Cellular Model of Neurodegeneration
proposals.motivation: Chronic neuroinflammation and oxidative stress are central, interconnected drivers of neurodegenerative pathologies like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease. Pre-2026 research by Aggarwal and others highlights Calebin A's potent anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor properties, including suppression of NF-κB, a master regulator of inflammation. However, its precise molecular mechanisms for mitigating oxidative stress specifically in neural contexts remain undefined, representing a critical gap for therapeutic translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Calebin A protects neuronal cells from inflammation-induced oxidative damage by concurrently activating the cytoprotective Nrf2/ARE antioxidant pathway and suppressing the pro-inflammatory NF-κB signaling cascade.
proposals.method: We will employ an in vitro model using SH-SY5Y neuroblastoma cells subjected to TNF-α-induced inflammation to mimic a neurodegenerative microenvironment. Specific molecular mechanisms will be elucidated via: 1) Western blot and immunofluorescence to quantify changes in NF-κB (p65 nuclear translocation), Nrf2, and downstream targets (HO-1, NQO1); 2) siRNA-mediated knockdown of Nrf2 to confirm pathway necessity; 3) measurement of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and mitochondrial membrane potential using fluorescent probes (DCFDA, JC-1); and 4) assessment of autophagy flux via LC3-II immunoblotting in the presence of lysosomal inhibitors, per established guidelines, to determine if autophagy contributes to the protective effect.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed mechanistic map of how Calebin A coordinates anti-inflammatory and antioxidant responses in a neuronal model. It will establish whether its neuroprotective potential is mediated through the Nrf2/ARE axis, offering a novel, multi-target therapeutic strategy against neurodegeneration. The work will also clarify the role of autophagy in this context.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A's anti-NF-κB activity in cancer is known, its direct engagement of the Nrf2 pathway in neural systems is unexplored. The novelty lies in investigating the compound's dual-pathway modulation (NF-κB suppression and Nrf2 activation) specifically to resolve inflammation-driven oxidative stress, a key nexus in neurodegeneration.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Resolution Analysis of Calebin A on Inflammation-Induced Autophagy and Epithelial-to-Mesenchymal Transition in Neuronal and Glial Co-Cultures
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation is a known driver of neurodegenerative diseases and brain tumor progression, involving complex crosstalk between neuronal and glial cells. Pre-2026 work by Aggarwal's group shows Calebin A has potent anti-inflammatory and anti-tumor effects in colorectal cancer, suppressing inflammation-induced EMT and modulating autophagy. However, its effects on the central nervous system, particularly at the single-cell level where cellular heterogeneity dictates disease outcomes, remain completely unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Calebin A will exert cell-type-specific protective effects in an inflammation-challenged neural microenvironment by differentially modulating pro-survival autophagy in neurons and suppressing inflammation-associated, pro-tumorigenic EMT signatures in glial cells at single-cell resolution.
proposals.method: We will establish co-cultures of human iPSC-derived neurons and glial cells (astrocytes/microglia) and induce inflammation using TNF-α/IL-1β. Treatment with Calebin A will be followed by single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics Chromium platform). Data analysis will employ the Seurat pipeline (v4) for clustering, differential expression, and trajectory inference. Cell-type-specific responses will be quantified for autophagy-related genes (following guidelines from autophagy assay interpretations) and EMT markers. Validation will use high-content imaging for LC3 puncta (autophagic flux) and EMT marker localization.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of Calebin A's action on distinct neural cell types, directly linking its known anti-inflammatory properties to cell-specific modulation of autophagy and EMT. It will establish a foundational mechanistic understanding of its potential neuroprotective and anti-gliomagenic effects, bridging the gap between cancer biology and neuroinflammation research.
proposals.novelty: While Calebin A's effects on EMT and inflammation are documented in bulk cancer cell studies, applying single-cell genomics to decode its cell-type-specific actions in a complex neural co-culture system under inflammatory stress is a novel and critical advancement. This approach moves beyond population averages to uncover heterogeneous cellular responses, a key frontier in phytochemical research pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Unifying Mechanism of Action: Investigating Calebin A's Core Inhibition of NF-κB-Driven Inflammatory Signaling Across Cancer, Diabetes, and Autoimmune Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Chronic inflammation, driven by dysregulated NF-κB signaling, is a well-established pre-2026 pathogenic nexus linking cancer, type 2 diabetes, and autoimmune disorders like rheumatoid arthritis. Despite distinct clinical presentations, shared upstream inflammatory mediators suggest a potential common therapeutic target. Dr. Aggarwal's prior work on Calebin A, a natural NF-κB inhibitor with anti-tumor and anti-inflammatory properties, provides a unique tool to test this paradigm of a 'one mechanism fits all' core pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary therapeutic mechanism of Calebin A across disparate diseases is the fundamental suppression of a core NF-κB-mediated inflammatory and stress-response program, which manifests as reduced tumor progression, improved insulin sensitivity, and attenuated autoimmune pathology through downstream modulation of disease-specific pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-disease cellular platform: human colorectal cancer cells (HCT-116), pancreatic beta-cells (INS-1) under glucolipotoxic stress, and peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from rheumatoid arthritis patients. Using standardized pre-2026 assays (western blot, EMSA, luciferase reporter, cytokine multiplex), we will quantify Calebin A's effect on canonical NF-κB activation (p65 nuclear translocation, IκBα degradation) in each model. Downstream, we will measure disease-specific readouts: EMT markers (vimentin, E-cadherin) in cancer, insulin secretion and AKT phosphorylation in beta-cells, and Th17/Treg cell polarization in PBMCs, correlating all to the degree of NF-κB inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct experimental evidence for or against a unifying anti-inflammatory mechanism of a natural compound across three major disease classes. It will clarify whether Calebin A's efficacy is rooted in a common upstream target, informing its potential as a broad-spectrum therapeutic agent. The study will also establish a rigorous comparative methodological framework for evaluating 'pan-disease' drug mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While NF-κB's role in individual diseases is known, systematically testing a single agent's core mechanism across cancer, metabolic, and autoimmune models using identical pharmacological and molecular endpoints is novel. It moves beyond association to direct mechanistic comparison, challenging the siloed disease model in pharmacotherapy.

proposals.title: Correlating Calebin A-induced modulation of NF-κB, STAT3, and autophagy with clinical biomarkers and outcomes in a phase I/II trial for colorectal cancer.
proposals.motivation: Calebin A exhibits pleiotropic anti-cancer effects in pre-clinical models, targeting key inflammatory pathways (NF-κB, STAT3) and autophagy associated with tumor progression and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT).
